target.name: Tempo
target.founder: Nima Rahmatian, Kelvin Randhir, Joerg Petrasch, Alessandro Bo

methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series A → next-stage step-up multiplier (2.0x) to $50M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Climate & Energy (found 0 same-stage, 0 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 9 same-sector peers exist in Climate & Energy, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Climate & Bio-Manufacturing

data.narrative:
Tempo is a Series A company in Climate & Energy founded by Nima Rahmatian, Kelvin Randhir, Joerg Petrasch, and Alessandro Bo. The company has raised $50M to date. The Climate & Energy sector comp universe includes 9 private peers and 1 public company (LNZA), with notable private comparables including Helion, Redwood Materials, QuantumScape, KoBolt Metals, and Electric Hydrogen.

Stage-progression analysis is the primary applicable method given data constraints. Using total capital raised ($50M) as a valuation proxy and applying a 2.0x step-up multiplier typical of Series A advancement yields an implied valuation of $100M. This method rests on the assumption that Tempo's next-stage financing will reflect standard market multiples for the sector. Peer-median triangulation was not applicable; while nine Climate & Energy peers exist in the broader dataset, none met the narrow filtering criteria (same stage, adjacent stage, and similar valuation tier simultaneously), and deploying an all-sector median would introduce material bias.

The analysis supports a valuation range of $85M to $115M, with a median point of $100M. This range reflects the single applicable method and carries meaningful uncertainty. Key limitations include reliance on a single triangulation technique, insufficient narrow-set peer data for cross-validation, and the proxy nature of using total raised as a valuation anchor. Material upside or downside could emerge with more detailed operating metrics or closer stage-matched comparables.
